title: Improve todo list rendering in agent output for readability
body:
## UX: Todo list output formatting improvement

The current todo list rendering in agent output is hard to read. It uses plain text with inline markdown checkboxes that get compressed and are difficult to scan.

### Current Format (Problem)

```
Updated the to-do list
Fix unconditional response.json() calls in App.tsx (root cause of JSON parse errors)\n- [x] Create comprehensive Playwright E2E test suite for data correctness, UI elements, styling\n- [-] Fix studio-data-and-ui.spec.ts syntax errors and service ID issues\n - [x] Add missing `});` to close `navigateAndWait` function\n - [x] Add missing `});` to close first test\n - [x] Change `service-studio` → `service-api` (studio at index 0 is sliced out)\n - [x] Fix `navigateAndWait` to pass hash URLs directly (was stripping `#`)\n - [ ] Rebuild and re-run tests\n - [ ] Fix remaining failures\n- [ ] Verify against Specsmith governance
```

Issues:
- Sub-items use inconsistent indentation (space vs tab)
- Long parent items run into child items
- No visual separation between top-level and nested items
- Status indicators (`[x]`, `[-]`, `[ ]`) are not visually distinct
- Paused state info is appended without clear formatting

### Proposed Format (Solution)

```
## Updated the to-do list

### Fix unconditional response.json() calls in App.tsx
- [x] Create comprehensive Playwright E2E test suite
- [x] Add missing `});` to close `navigateAndWait` function
- [x] Add missing `});` to close first test
- [x] Change `service-studio` → `service-api`
- [x] Fix `navigateAndWait` to pass hash URLs directly
- [ ] Rebuild and re-run tests
- [ ] Fix remaining failures

### Verify against Specsmith governance
- [ ] Run governance checkpoint
```

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each top-level todo item gets its own `###` heading or clear separator
- [ ] Sub-items use consistent 2-space indentation under parent
- [ ] Status badges or emoji indicators for visual distinction:
  - `[x]` → ✅ completed
  - `[-]` → 🔄 in progress
  - `[ ]` → ⏳ pending
- [ ] Paused/resumed state shown in a collapsible or clearly separated block
- [ ] Long item titles are truncated with ellipsis when exceeding 60 chars
- [ ] Format is consistent across all agent modes (code, architect, debug, etc.)

### Implementation Notes

- Modify the `update_todo_list` tool output formatting in agent response generation
- Consider a dedicated rendering function for todo lists in agent output
- Ensure markdown compatibility across all output surfaces (terminal, IDE, web UI)
- Test with deeply nested todo lists (3+ levels)

### Labels

`ux`, `enhancement`, `agent-output`, `readability`

title: Model generates malformed diff format: :start_line: marker in REPLACE section causes edit failures
body:
## Bug: Malformed diff format from model tool use

Zoo Code (and potentially other AI agents) is generating diffs with `:start_line:` markers in the **REPLACE** section instead of the **SEARCH** section, causing `apply_diff` failures.

### Error Details

```
ERROR: Invalid line marker ':start_line:' found in REPLACE section at line 4

Line markers (:start_line: and :end_line:) are only allowed in SEARCH sections.

CORRECT FORMAT:
<<<<<<< SEARCH
:start_line:5
content to find
=======
replacement content
>>>>>>> REPLACE

INCORRECT FORMAT:
<<<<<<< SEARCH
content to find
=======
:start_line:5    <-- Invalid location
replacement content
>>>>>>> REPLACE
```

### Affected File

- `c:\Users\trist\Development\Layer1Labs\chronofabric\chronofabric-ip\scripts\bootstrap.py`
- Truncation at line 538 inside `platform_map` dict

### Environment

- Date/time: 2026-07-16T19:22:19.431Z
- Extension version: 3.68.0
- Provider: litellm (proxy)
- Model: ChronoCortex-LLM

### Root Cause Analysis Needed

1. **Model training gap**: The model does not correctly associate `:start_line:` with the SEARCH block
2. **Tool-use instruction clarity**: The diff format spec may need reinforcement in system prompts
3. **Truncation handling**: When files are truncated, the model may lose context about where markers belong

### Reproduction Steps

1. Open a file with a large dict (e.g., `platform_map` with 500+ lines)
2. Ask the model to replace a truncated/incomplete section
3. Model generates diff with `:start_line:` in REPLACE block
4. `apply_diff` tool rejects the edit

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] System prompt or tool instructions explicitly reinforce `:start_line:` placement in SEARCH blocks only
- [ ] Model generates valid diff format for truncated file edits
- [ ] Fallback strategy documented (e.g., `write_to_file` when diffs fail repeatedly)
- [ ] Error recovery flow: on `apply_diff` failure, retry with corrected format or switch to full file write

### Mitigation for Users (Workaround)

Until fixed, users can:
- Break large edits into smaller, targeted changes
- Use `write_to_file` for complete file rewrites instead of `apply_diff` for truncated sections
- Manually specify the exact content to search for without line markers

### Labels

`bug`, `tool-use`, `model-training`, `diff-format`, `zoo-code`

title: Define optimal invocation strategy: MCP vs /slash commands vs skills vs direct CLI
body:
## Epic: Invocation Strategy Optimization

Specsmith supports multiple invocation mechanisms. This ticket defines when each should be used for optimal UX, reliability, and cross-environment compatibility.

### Invocation Mechanisms

1. **MCP (Model Context Protocol)** — `governance_preflight`, `governance_audit`, `governance_checkpoint`, etc.
   - Best for: AI agent-to-agent communication, structured governance decisions, programmatic access
   - Environments: Cursor, VS Code extensions, any MCP-compatible client
   - Pros: Structured JSON I/O, governance-enforced, traceable
   - Cons: Requires MCP server running, limited to MCP-defined tools

2. **Slash Commands** — `/specsmith save`, `/specsmith audit`, `/specsmith status`, etc. (REPL)
   - Best for: Interactive sessions, quick governance operations, human operators
   - Environments: Nexus REPL, any terminal with REPL mode
   - Pros: Simple syntax, built into specsmith CLI, no extra setup
   - Cons: REPL-bound, not scriptable in non-interactive contexts

3. **Skills** — Loaded via `skill` tool (e.g., `execution`, `git`, `release`, `testing-*`)
   - Best for: Complex, multi-step workflows with specific domain procedures
   - Environments: AI agents with skill-loading capability
   - Pros: Rich procedural instructions, domain-specific guidance
   - Cons: Skill must be loaded first, adds context overhead

4. **Direct CLI** — `specsmith preflight`, `specsmith audit`, `specsmith sync`, `specsmith checkpoint`, etc.
   - Best for: Scripting, CI/CD pipelines, automation, shell scripts
   - Environments: Any shell (bash, pwsh, cmd), CI runners
   - Pros: Fully scriptable, no server required, works in headless environments
   - Cons: Less structured output, no built-in governance enforcement without flags

### Decision Matrix

| Scenario | Preferred | Fallback |
|---|---|---|
| AI agent in IDE (Cursor/VS Code) | MCP tools | Slash commands |
| Interactive terminal session | Slash commands | Direct CLI |
| Complex domain workflow | Skill | Direct CLI |
| CI/CD pipeline | Direct CLI | MCP (if server available) |
| Headless/server environments | Direct CLI | — |
| Cross-platform automation | Direct CLI | — |
| Governance-enforced decisions | MCP tools | Direct CLI with `--json` |

### Integration Considerations

- **Zoo Code**: Prefers MCP for governance decisions, slash commands for quick ops
- **Local LLM agents**: Direct CLI (no MCP server needed)
- **Remote CI runners**: Direct CLI with `SPECSMITH_ALLOW_NON_PIPX=1` as needed
- **Multi-agent coordination**: MCP for structured handoffs

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Decision matrix documented in AGENTS.md or docs/
- [ ] Clear guidance on when each mechanism is appropriate
- [ ] Fallback strategies documented
- [ ] Environment compatibility matrix (Windows/Linux/macOS, IDE/headless)
- [ ] Zoo Code integration notes included

### Labels

`epic`, `documentation`, `ux`, `cross-platform`

title: Windows pipx CLI startup can hang in Click/gettext before command dispatch
body:
## Objective

Prevent `specsmith` from hanging or taking unbounded time during CLI import and command registration on Windows before the requested subcommand is dispatched.

This was observed with a pipx-installed Specsmith using Python 3.12 on Windows PowerShell. It is not currently covered by another open issue. It is related to the cross-platform audit in #330, but is a distinct public-CLI startup defect. It follows the test-evidence contract in #337.

## Observed reproduction

Working directory:

```text
C:\Users\trist\Development\Creative Racing\crscale
```

Command:

```powershell
specsmith load
```

Observed stack at manual interruption:

```text
File "...\specsmith\cli.py", line 13894, in <module>
  from specsmith.commands.reporting import register_reporting_commands
File "...\specsmith\commands\reporting.py", line 494, in <module>
  @click.option("--project-dir", type=click.Path(exists=True), default=".")
File "...\click\types.py", line 1073, in __init__
  self.name = _("path")
File "...\gettext.py", line 617, in gettext
  return dgettext(_current_domain, message)
File "...\gettext.py", line 523, in translation
  mofiles = find(domain, localedir, languages, all=True)
File "...\gettext.py", line 507, in find
  if os.path.exists(mofile):
KeyboardInterrupt
```

Environment visible in the traceback:

- Windows PowerShell
- pipx executable: `C:\Users\trist\.local\bin\specsmith.exe`
- pipx environment: `C:\Users\trist\AppData\Local\pipx\pipx\venvs\specsmith`
- Python 3.12 runtime under `AppData\Local\Python\pythoncore-3.12-64`
- repository path contains spaces

The final `KeyboardInterrupt` means the user manually stopped a startup hang; it is not the underlying error.

## Current-state evidence

On current `main`:

- `specsmith.cli` eagerly imports `specsmith.commands.reporting` during module initialization;
- `reporting.py` constructs Click decorators such as `click.Path(exists=True)` at import time;
- Click initializes translated type names through Python `gettext` while those decorators are created;
- command registration occurs before Click can dispatch `load`;
- therefore a slow or pathological locale lookup in an unrelated command module can block every CLI command.

The import is wrapped in `except Exception`, but `KeyboardInterrupt` derives from `BaseException`, not `Exception`, and the import has already blocked before normal command handling can proceed. Broadly swallowing import exceptions is also not an acceptable fix because it can silently remove commands.

## Required investigation

Reproduce and profile startup using the same installation topology:

1. Windows hosted runner or Windows VM;
2. Python 3.12 and at least one other supported Python version;
3. pipx installation, not only an editable virtual environment;
4. PowerShell invocation;
5. working directory containing spaces;
6. representative `LANG`, `LC_ALL`, `LANGUAGE`, and Windows locale combinations;
7. cold and warm filesystem/cache states.

Capture timing and stack evidence for:

- Python import time;
- Click command-tree construction;
- gettext domain/language resolution;
- filesystem probes generated by gettext;
- endpoint-security or unavailable-drive interactions where reproducible;
- difference between importing `specsmith.cli`, importing `reporting.py`, and constructing one `click.Path` instance.

Do not assume `click.Path` itself is defective until isolated. The stack shows where the thread was interrupted, not necessarily the sole root cause.

## Required behavior

- Every basic CLI command must dispatch within a bounded startup time under supported Windows/pipx configurations.
- `specsmith load` must not import or construct unrelated heavyweight command modules before dispatch unless required by the chosen CLI architecture.
- Command registration failures must be visible and actionable; commands must not disappear silently behind broad `except Exception: pass` blocks.
- Locale initialization must be deterministic and must not trigger unbounded filesystem searches.
- Startup behavior must remain compatible with supported localization behavior in Click.
- The fix must not special-case the user's path or disable localization globally without evidence and documentation.
- The implementation should prefer modular/lazy command loading or another bounded registration strategy if eager construction is the amplifying design flaw.
- Add a startup diagnostic mode or documented profiling command that identifies slow command-module imports without requiring the CLI to finish normal dispatch.

## Suggested implementation directions to evaluate

These are investigation candidates, not predetermined solutions:

1. Lazy-load command groups/modules only when their command namespace is selected.
2. Move decorator/type construction out of global import paths where practical.
3. Replace the broad reporting import suppression with explicit registration and actionable failure reporting.
4. Cache or constrain locale resolution only if profiling proves gettext lookup is the root cause.
5. Pin or constrain Click only if an upstream regression is demonstrated and linked.
6. Ensure packaged locale/environment metadata does not point gettext at invalid, network, or excessively broad paths.

## Named tests and binary pass/fail conditions

### CLI-START-001 — Windows pipx load startup

**Fixture:** install the built wheel with pipx on Windows using Python 3.12; invoke `specsmith load` from a project path containing spaces.

**Pass:** the process reaches command execution or a normal project-level response within the configured startup budget and never requires manual interruption.

**Fail:** startup times out, remains in import/Click/gettext initialization, or produces no bounded diagnostic.

### CLI-START-002 — Basic command startup matrix

**Fixture:** invoke `--help`, `--version`, `load`, `save`, `doctor`, and one reporting command from a pipx install.

**Pass:** every command dispatches within the startup budget; unrelated command modules cannot block `--version`, `--help`, or `load`.

**Fail:** any command hangs during command-tree construction or imports unrelated optional functionality before dispatch.

### CLI-START-003 — Locale matrix

**Fixture:** supported Windows locale plus explicit unset, valid, invalid, and multi-entry values for `LANG`, `LC_ALL`, and `LANGUAGE`.

**Pass:** startup is bounded and diagnostics identify unsupported locale values without hanging.

**Fail:** any locale combination causes unbounded gettext path probing or silent command loss.

### CLI-START-004 — Cold-start filesystem behavior

**Fixture:** cold process with empty Python/Click caches and instrumentation of filesystem probes.

**Pass:** locale and command registration perform a bounded number of local probes and do not enumerate unavailable/network roots.

**Fail:** startup performs unbounded/repeated existence checks or depends on an unavailable drive/share.

### CLI-START-005 — Reporting isolation

**Fixture:** instrument imports while invoking `specsmith load`.

**Pass:** either `reporting.py` is not imported before `load` dispatch, or its registration is proven bounded below the startup budget.

**Fail:** an unrelated reporting command can block `load` indefinitely.

### CLI-START-006 — Registration failure visibility

**Fixture:** inject a deterministic import/registration error into an optional command module.

**Pass:** the CLI reports the failed module and remediation while preserving commands that can safely operate; no bare `pass` hides the defect.

**Fail:** the command silently disappears or all commands crash without identifying the registration failure.

### CLI-START-007 — KeyboardInterrupt handling

**Fixture:** interrupt during intentionally delayed command registration.

**Pass:** the process exits promptly with a clear interruption status and no corrupted state.

**Fail:** interruption is swallowed, misreported as a normal command error, or leaves persistent state half-written.

### CLI-START-008 — Supported Python matrix

**Fixture:** Windows pipx installs across supported Python versions, including 3.12.

**Pass:** startup semantics and budgets are equivalent across the supported matrix.

**Fail:** the defect is hidden by testing only editable installs or one Python version.

### CLI-START-009 — Import-time performance budget

**Fixture:** automated timing for `python -c "import specsmith.cli"` and real console-script dispatch.

**Pass:** both remain under an explicitly documented CI budget with a reasonable hosted-runner tolerance; regressions fail CI with per-module timing evidence.

**Fail:** no enforceable budget exists or timing failures provide no localization data.

### CLI-START-010 — Packaged-wheel parity

**Fixture:** compare source checkout, ordinary venv wheel install, and pipx wheel install.

**Pass:** all use the same registered command surface and none hang; differences are recorded and justified.

**Fail:** tests pass only in the source checkout while the published installation topology remains broken.

### CLI-START-011 — Path-character coverage

**Fixture:** working directories containing spaces, Unicode, parentheses, and long Windows paths within supported limits.

**Pass:** CLI startup and command dispatch remain bounded and path-correct.

**Fail:** path spelling changes locale lookup behavior or prevents dispatch.

### CLI-START-012 — No silent command loss

**Fixture:** enumerate the expected public command surface after startup.

**Pass:** every expected command is present, or an unavailable optional command has an explicit recorded reason.

**Fail:** broad exception handling silently removes a command group.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] The reported Windows/pipx reproduction no longer hangs.
- [ ] Root cause is demonstrated with timing/stack evidence rather than inferred solely from the interrupted frame.
- [ ] CLI dispatch has an explicit, automated startup budget.
- [ ] Unrelated command modules cannot indefinitely block `load`, `--help`, or `--version`.
- [ ] Command registration errors are not silently swallowed.
- [ ] Published-wheel and pipx topology are tested on Windows.
- [ ] CLI-START-001 through CLI-START-012 pass with machine-readable evidence under #337.

## Relationship to other tickets

- #330: include this regression in the broader cross-platform release/bootstrap audit, but do not close this ticket based only on release CI portability.
- #313: shell selection happens after CLI startup and does not resolve this import-time hang.
- #341: Zoo settings lifecycle is unrelated except that its CLI entry points also require bounded startup.

## Non-goals

- Do not treat activation of the repository's `tests\.venv` after the failure as the fix; the failing `specsmith.exe` came from the pipx environment.
- Do not close the issue merely because retrying after caches warm happens to succeed.
- Do not remove reporting commands to make startup faster.
- Do not disable all localization without a verified compatibility decision.

title: Automate Zoo Code settings lifecycle and document manual-only integration steps
body:
## Objective

Make Specsmith automatically configure every Zoo Code setting required for governed operation whenever Zoo exposes a stable machine-editable interface. When a setting can only be changed manually, detect that limitation, report the exact remaining action to the user, and document it in both the main repository and Read the Docs.

This is a child of #335 and coordinates #329, #330, #331, #339, and #340. It follows the test contract in #337.

## Current-state assessment

Specsmith already has a Zoo Code integration lifecycle that manages reusable global rules, commands, skills, and the repo-local `.roo/mcp.json`. The existing RTD page is `docs/site/zoo-code-roo.md`, and `mkdocs.yml` already exposes it under Agentic Runtime → Agentic Client → Zoo Code / Roo Code.

However, Zoo Code also stores important behavior in extension/global settings, including:

- `autoCondenseContext` and `autoCondenseContextPercent`;
- `customCondensingPrompt`;
- `enableCheckpoints` and checkpoint timeout;
- `alwaysAllowMcp`, `alwaysAllowExecute`, and command allow/deny lists;
- provider profiles and per-profile thresholds;
- MCP enablement;
- custom modes and prompts;
- terminal-output/context settings.

The current Specsmith setup does not provide a complete settings ownership inventory, automated mutation plan, manual-only fallback report, or drift reconciliation for these settings.

## Core policy

1. **Automate by default.** If Zoo exposes a stable documented file, command, API, VS Code setting, extension storage adapter, or import format, Specsmith should configure it automatically.
2. **Do not automate by guessing.** Never edit undocumented opaque extension storage directly unless the format and lifecycle are explicitly supported and tested.
3. **Explain manual-only steps exactly.** When automation is unavailable, setup and doctor must show the precise UI path, setting name, required value, reason, verification step, and whether restart/reload is required.
4. **Preserve user-owned settings.** Modify only required Specsmith-managed keys. Back up or transactionally merge settings, and never overwrite unrelated profiles, modes, MCP servers, prompts, or user preferences.
5. **Make state explainable.** For every desired Zoo setting, report `managed`, `already-compliant`, `manual-required`, `unsupported-version`, `conflict`, or `not-applicable`.
6. **Fail closed for governance-critical gaps.** A manual-only setting that is required for #340 must prevent “fully governed” status until verified.

## Settings capability registry

Create a versioned registry describing each Zoo integration setting:

```yaml
settings:
  context.auto_condense:
    desired: false
    required_for: governed-context
    discovery:
      - zoo-settings-api
      - vscode-settings
      - import-export
    automation: preferred
    verification: runtime-probe
    manual:
      ui_path: Settings > Context Management
      label: Auto Condense Context
      expected: Off
      reload_required: false
```

Each entry must include:

- semantic Specsmith key;
- applicable Zoo versions;
- native Zoo key(s);
- desired/default value;
- whether required or recommended;
- permitted automation mechanisms;
- read-back verification method;
- conflict policy;
- rollback behavior;
- manual instructions;
- restart/reload requirement;
- associated issue/test IDs.

Avoid hard-coding scattered setting mutations throughout the integration module.

## Required automation workflow

`specsmith zoo-code setup` must:

1. detect Zoo/Roo installation and version where possible;
2. inventory available configuration surfaces;
3. resolve desired values through #339 global/project/local configuration;
4. apply all safely automatable settings;
5. preserve unrelated user state;
6. read back and verify applied values;
7. create a machine-readable integration state manifest;
8. output only remaining manual actions;
9. state whether the integration is fully governed, partially configured, or unmanaged;
10. recommend/reload the VS Code window only when necessary.

`specsmith zoo-code doctor` must:

- compare desired, detected, and applied values;
- identify drift and version incompatibility;
- automatically repair safe managed drift when `--fix` is used;
- produce exact manual steps for unfixable gaps;
- distinguish required failures from recommendations;
- expose JSON output suitable for agents and CI.

Suggested commands/options:

```text
specsmith zoo-code setup --project-dir .
specsmith zoo-code setup --dry-run
specsmith zoo-code setup --json
specsmith zoo-code doctor --project-dir .
specsmith zoo-code doctor --fix
specsmith zoo-code settings show
specsmith zoo-code settings explain <key>
specsmith zoo-code settings export-instructions
```

## Manual-action contract

When manual configuration is unavoidable, print a structured block such as:

```text
MANUAL ACTION REQUIRED: Disable Zoo Code Auto Condense
Reason: Zoo version 1.x does not expose this setting through a supported configuration API.
Required for: Specsmith governed context (#340)

1. Open VS Code.
2. Open Zoo Code.
3. Select Settings.
4. Open Context Management.
5. Turn “Auto Condense Context” off.
6. Reload the VS Code window only if Zoo requests it.
7. Run: specsmith zoo-code doctor --project-dir .

Verification: doctor must report context.auto_condense = compliant.
Until verified: integration status is PARTIALLY CONFIGURED; governed-context assurances are disabled.
```

Requirements:

- use exact current UI labels for the detected Zoo version;
- never say only “change this in settings”;
- include keyboard-command-palette alternatives where useful;
- include screenshots only when maintainable, with text instructions remaining authoritative;
- avoid exposing secrets;
- provide copyable values;
- localize only when reliable; English instructions are the baseline.

## Settings required for #340

At minimum, the registry/setup must manage or explicitly instruct for:

- Zoo native autonomous semantic auto-condense disabled/bypassed;
- Zoo default/custom CONDENSE prompt not used in healthy governed mode;
- Zoo sliding-window fallback behavior gated by the Specsmith adapter;
- MCP enabled and `specsmith-governance` installed;
- Specsmith context MCP tools allowed for all governed modes;
- all Specsmith custom modes routed through the context controller;
- workspace checkpoints labeled/configured separately from semantic checkpoints;
- profile thresholds coordinated so Specsmith receives telemetry before Zoo reaches a hard limit;
- required executable/MCP permissions;
- unmanaged opt-out clearly represented.

## Provider/profile settings

Coordinate with #331:

- automatically create/update the Specsmith-managed local-LLM LiteLLM profile where Zoo's supported import/config mechanism allows it;
- preserve user-created profiles;
- do not write secrets directly into exportable settings;
- use environment/keychain references where supported;
- when API keys or UI-only secure fields require manual input, provide exact instructions and a verification probe that does not reveal the key;
- detect endpoint reachability and model aliases separately from configuration presence.

## Ownership and rollback

- Record every key Specsmith changed and its prior value in a local managed-state manifest.
- `uninstall` should restore prior values only when the current value still equals the Specsmith-managed value; otherwise preserve user modifications and report them.
- Setup must be idempotent.
- Upgrades must migrate registry/schema versions deterministically.
- Concurrent VS Code/Specsmith writes must use conflict detection rather than blind overwrite.
- Global, project, and profile settings must not be confused.

## Documentation requirements

### Main repository

Update or add a prominent integration document in the repository, preferably:

- `docs/site/zoo-code-roo.md` as the canonical user guide used by RTD;
- a concise link/summary in `README.md` under Integrations or Agent Integrations;
- optional developer architecture notes under `docs/` if the adapter/settings registry needs deeper implementation documentation.

The Zoo guide must document:

1. supported Zoo/Roo versions;
2. architecture and ownership boundary;
3. what Specsmith configures automatically;
4. what may require manual configuration;
5. exact setup and doctor commands;
6. governed-context behavior from #340;
7. semantic checkpoint vs workspace snapshot terminology;
8. local model/LiteLLM profile setup;
9. global/project/local configuration ownership from #339;
10. troubleshooting and recovery;
11. uninstall/rollback behavior;
12. privacy/secrets behavior;
13. managed vs unmanaged status indicators.

### Read the Docs

- Keep the page under the existing `mkdocs.yml` Agentic Runtime → Agentic Client integration area.
- Ensure navigation title remains clear: `Zoo Code / Roo Code`.
- Add cross-links from `agent-integrations.md`, `configuration.md`, `doctor.md`, and relevant context/ESDB pages.
- Validate internal links and build the docs in CI.
- The RTD page and repository source must be the same canonical Markdown, not separately maintained copies.

## Machine-readable status

Produce status such as:

```json
{
  "integration": "zoo-code",
  "zoo_version": "...",
  "status": "partial_manual_required",
  "governed_context": false,
  "settings": [
    {
      "key": "context.auto_condense",
      "desired": false,
      "actual": true,
      "state": "manual-required",
      "required": true,
      "instructions_id": "ZOO-MANUAL-CONTEXT-001"
    }
  ]
}
```

This output should be consumable by agents without parsing prose.

## Named tests and binary pass/fail conditions

### ZSET-001 — Safe automatic configuration

**Pass:** setup modifies every supported required key, preserves unrelated settings, reads back the values, and reports no unnecessary manual actions.

**Fail:** required supported keys remain unset or unrelated user state changes.

### ZSET-002 — Idempotent setup

**Pass:** a second setup run produces zero semantic changes and identical managed-state output.

**Fail:** repeated runs create duplicates, rewrite files, or reset user values.

### ZSET-003 — Manual-only instruction quality

**Pass:** an unsupported/UI-only fixture produces exact UI path, label, value, reason, verification command, reload requirement, and governed-status impact.

**Fail:** instructions are vague, incomplete, or cannot be verified.

### ZSET-004 — Required manual gap fails closed

**Pass:** an unverified manual-only governance-critical setting results in `partial_manual_required` and no full #340 assurance.

**Fail:** setup/doctor reports healthy governed mode despite the gap.

### ZSET-005 — Version-specific mapping

**Pass:** supported Zoo versions resolve the correct native key/UI label, and unknown versions produce a bounded unsupported-version result.

**Fail:** Specsmith blindly writes an old key or presents stale instructions.

### ZSET-006 — User-setting preservation

**Pass:** custom profiles, unrelated MCP servers, prompts, modes, and preferences remain byte/semantically unchanged.

**Fail:** setup replaces or removes unmanaged state.

### ZSET-007 — Drift detection and repair

**Pass:** changing a managed value is detected; `doctor --fix` repairs automatable drift and prints manual instructions otherwise.

**Fail:** drift is missed or repaired through an unsupported mechanism.

### ZSET-008 — Secrets

**Pass:** secret values are never printed, committed, added to the managed manifest, or copied into non-secret Zoo settings.

**Fail:** any credential leaks through logs/config/docs/tests.

### ZSET-009 — Uninstall rollback

**Pass:** uninstall restores recorded prior values when safe, preserves post-setup user edits, and removes only Specsmith-owned state.

**Fail:** user changes are overwritten or unrelated state is removed.

### ZSET-010 — Concurrent-write conflict

**Pass:** an external settings change between read and write causes an explicit conflict/retry rather than silent overwrite.

**Fail:** last writer silently wins.

### ZSET-011 — Governed-context settings

**Pass:** all #340-required settings are compliant or explicitly manual-blocked, and instrumentation proves Zoo native summarization is not reachable in a healthy governed session.

**Fail:** settings appear configured while Zoo retains autonomous semantic control.

### ZSET-012 — Provider profile automation

**Pass:** the Specsmith LiteLLM profile is created/updated through a supported mechanism without exposing credentials and without altering user profiles.

**Fail:** setup requires avoidable manual profile entry or overwrites profiles.

### ZSET-013 — Cross-platform paths

**Pass:** Linux, Windows, and macOS discover and modify only the correct supported config surfaces.

**Fail:** path assumptions cause missed or wrong-user writes.

### ZSET-014 — Repository documentation

**Pass:** README links to the canonical Zoo guide and accurately distinguishes automatic and manual configuration.

**Fail:** integration behavior exists without discoverable repository documentation.

### ZSET-015 — RTD integration documentation

**Pass:** `docs/site/zoo-code-roo.md` is in `mkdocs.yml`, cross-links resolve, and the documentation build succeeds.

**Fail:** RTD omits the guide, uses stale duplicated content, or contains broken links.

### ZSET-016 — Instruction verification loop

**Pass:** after the user completes a manual step, doctor detects the compliant value and clears the action without requiring hidden state.

**Fail:** the user cannot prove completion or the warning remains incorrectly.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Every relevant Zoo setting has a registry entry and ownership classification.
- [ ] Setup automatically applies all safely supported settings.
- [ ] Manual-only requirements produce exact, version-aware, verifiable instructions.
- [ ] Governance-critical manual gaps block full governed status.
- [ ] Doctor detects drift and supports safe repair.
- [ ] Setup/upgrade/uninstall preserve user-owned settings and secrets.
- [ ] #340 context-controller requirements are represented explicitly.
- [ ] `docs/site/zoo-code-roo.md` is expanded as the canonical repository/RTD guide.
- [ ] README and related RTD pages cross-link to the guide.
- [ ] ZSET-001 through ZSET-016 pass with #337 machine-readable evidence.

## Non-goals

- Do not edit opaque undocumented Zoo extension databases merely to avoid a manual step.
- Do not promise automation where Zoo exposes no stable integration surface.
- Do not maintain separate README and RTD copies of the full guide.
- Do not store Zoo/provider credentials in tracked Specsmith configuration.
- Do not silently claim the integration is governed when required settings cannot be verified.

## Agent execution notes

First create a complete matrix of Zoo settings, storage surfaces, supported versions, and ownership. Prefer official import/export or extension APIs over direct storage edits. Add read-back verification before enabling automatic writes. Implement the registry and dry-run report before mutation. Treat documentation and manual instructions as tested product behavior, not an afterthought.

title: Unify Specsmith global, project, and local configuration schemas
body:
## Objective

Create one documented, versioned configuration system for Specsmith that cleanly separates:

1. global user and machine preferences;
2. tracked project policy and metadata;
3. untracked project-local/machine-local overrides;
4. environment and command-line overrides.

Consolidate the current fragmented configuration paths without losing backward compatibility. This is required by #338 and #336 and follows the test contract in #337.

## Current-state assessment

Specsmith already has a substantial Pydantic `ProjectConfig` used for project scaffolding and a canonical tracked project manifest at `docs/SPECSMITH.yml`. However, other code separately reads `.specsmith/governance/config.yaml` and falls back to `.specsmith/config.yml`. There is no single versioned global user configuration schema or centralized precedence/resolution API.

The result is ambiguity over which file owns project metadata, governance policy, runtime preferences, ESDB settings, Zoo Code settings, identity, and machine-specific values.

## Configuration layers and precedence

Define one resolver with this precedence, highest first:

1. explicit CLI flags;
2. environment variables;
3. untracked project-local config;
4. tracked project config;
5. global user config;
6. built-in defaults.

Every resolved value must expose its source layer through `config explain`.

## Canonical locations

Use platform-native global config directories:

- Linux: `${XDG_CONFIG_HOME:-~/.config}/specsmith/config.yml`
- macOS: `~/Library/Application Support/Specsmith/config.yml` unless XDG is explicitly selected
- Windows: `%APPDATA%\Specsmith\config.yml`

Project files:

- `docs/SPECSMITH.yml` — tracked, reviewable project policy and metadata;
- `.specsmith/local.yml` — untracked local overrides for one checkout/worktree;
- secrets remain outside these files or use references to environment variables/keyrings.

Do not use `.specsmith/config.yml` as a second ambiguous tracked project schema after migration. Preserve it only as a legacy input until migrated.

## Schema separation

Create versioned Pydantic models such as:

- `GlobalConfig`
- `ProjectConfig`
- `LocalConfig`
- `ResolvedConfig`
- nested sections for identity, ESDB, context governance, Zoo Code, providers, verification, paths, telemetry, and privacy.

Fields must explicitly declare which layers may set them. For example:

- project name/type/requirements policy: tracked project layer;
- preferred provider, prompt caching, default identity alias: global layer, locally overridable;
- replica ID, local endpoint reachability, machine paths: local/global machine state, never tracked project policy;
- API keys and credentials: secret references only, never plaintext tracked config.

## Required behavior

1. Add a single config loader/resolver used by CLI, governance, ESDB, context orchestration, Zoo integration, and identity resolution.
2. Add schema versions and deterministic migrations.
3. Detect duplicate/conflicting legacy files and fail with actionable diagnostics rather than silently selecting an arbitrary value.
4. Preserve comments where practical; otherwise create backups and provide a migration report.
5. Add commands:

```text
specsmith config paths
specsmith config show [--resolved]
specsmith config get <key>
specsmith config set <key> <value> [--global|--project|--local]
specsmith config unset <key> [--global|--project|--local]
specsmith config explain <key>
specsmith config validate
specsmith config migrate
```

6. `show --resolved` must redact secrets and display source provenance for each value.
7. Unknown keys must either fail validation or be preserved in an explicitly namespaced extensions section; never silently drop them.
8. Project config must be deterministic and merge-friendly: stable key ordering, UTF-8, LF normalization, no timestamps or machine-specific paths.
9. Local config must be automatically added to generated `.gitignore` files.
10. Global and local writes must be atomic and permission-conscious.
11. Support configuration profiles only when their semantics are explicit; avoid duplicating Zoo Code profile storage inside Specsmith.
12. Document which values belong in each layer and provide minimal examples.

## Migration mapping

- canonical scaffold/project fields from `docs/SPECSMITH.yml` remain in the tracked project model;
- legacy root `scaffold.yml` migrates to `docs/SPECSMITH.yml`;
- `.specsmith/governance/config.yaml` governance-specific values migrate into a namespaced project section when tracked policy, or local section when machine-specific;
- `.specsmith/config.yml` is classified field-by-field and migrated to project/global/local as appropriate;
- conflicts require an explicit report and may not be silently overwritten.

## Named tests and binary pass/fail conditions

### CONFIG-001 — Precedence

**Pass:** CLI > environment > local > project > global > defaults for every tested field, and `config explain` reports the winning source.

**Fail:** a lower layer overrides a higher layer or source reporting is incorrect.

### CONFIG-002 — Platform paths

**Pass:** Linux, macOS, and Windows resolve canonical global paths without embedding another platform's separators or locations.

**Fail:** platform paths differ from the declared policy or require manual path editing.

### CONFIG-003 — Project determinism

**Pass:** writing the same project config twice is byte-identical and produces no Git diff.

**Fail:** ordering, timestamps, newline conversion, or machine values create drift.

### CONFIG-004 — Local isolation

**Pass:** `.specsmith/local.yml` overrides one worktree, is ignored by Git, and does not change another worktree's resolved config.

**Fail:** local state is committed or leaks between worktrees.

### CONFIG-005 — Legacy migration

**Pass:** fixtures containing all legacy config locations migrate without value loss, create a report/backup, and a second migration is zero-diff.

**Fail:** values disappear, move to the wrong layer, or migration is non-idempotent.

### CONFIG-006 — Conflicting legacy values

**Pass:** contradictory values in legacy files produce an explicit conflict requiring resolution.

**Fail:** one value silently wins.

### CONFIG-007 — Secret handling

**Pass:** resolved output redacts secret values, tracked files contain only references, and doctor detects plaintext credentials in tracked config.

**Fail:** credentials appear in output, logs, fixtures, or committed files.

### CONFIG-008 — Unknown keys

**Pass:** unknown keys are rejected with location-aware diagnostics or preserved under a documented extension namespace.

**Fail:** unknown keys are silently ignored or removed.

### CONFIG-009 — Concurrent project edits

**Pass:** two branches changing different project fields merge cleanly; conflicting same-field changes remain normal visible Git conflicts and are never auto-resolved by machine-local precedence.

**Fail:** generated rewrites cause whole-file conflicts or silently choose one branch.

### CONFIG-010 — Consumer parity

**Pass:** CLI, governance store, ESDB, context orchestration, Zoo integration, and identity resolver all receive the same resolved values and provenance.

**Fail:** any subsystem reads a legacy file independently and obtains a different value.

### CONFIG-011 — Atomic writes and recovery

**Pass:** interrupted writes leave either the old or new valid file, with recovery diagnostics and no partial YAML.

**Fail:** truncation or malformed configuration is accepted.

### CONFIG-012 — Manual editing round-trip

**Pass:** a documented hand-edited config validates and survives load/write without semantic changes.

**Fail:** valid user changes are overwritten or normalized into different semantics.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] One resolver and precedence model governs all Specsmith configuration consumers.
- [ ] Versioned global, project, and local schemas exist.
- [ ] `docs/SPECSMITH.yml` remains the canonical tracked project file.
- [ ] `.specsmith/local.yml` is the canonical ignored project-local override.
- [ ] Global paths are platform-native.
- [ ] Legacy config files migrate deterministically and idempotently.
- [ ] Secrets are referenced/redacted, not stored in tracked config.
- [ ] CONFIG-001 through CONFIG-012 exist and pass with machine-readable #337 evidence.
- [ ] Documentation clearly explains ownership, precedence, paths, and examples.

## Non-goals

- Do not create one giant file containing secrets, runtime databases, and project policy.
- Do not let global preferences silently override tracked project safety policy unless the field explicitly allows it.
- Do not duplicate provider secrets or Zoo Code-managed credentials.
- Do not retain multiple active project config files after migration.

## Agent execution notes

Inventory every direct config-file read before implementing. Introduce the resolver first, migrate consumers incrementally, and add a temporary diagnostic for legacy direct reads. Keep configuration semantics separate from ESDB records: config determines policy; ESDB records actions and evidence.

title: Add layered Specsmith user, agent, and replica identity awareness
body:
## Objective

Give Specsmith stable, explainable awareness of the human user, executing agent, service principal, replica/worktree, and session responsible for every governed action. Auto-detect useful identity signals, persist a global default, allow project-local overrides, and let users inspect or manually change the resolved identity without silently conflating display names with identity.

This is a focused child of #336 and depends on the layered configuration foundation in #339. It follows the verification contract in #337.

## Identity model

Define distinct identity dimensions:

- `actor_id`: stable human or service-principal identity responsible for the action;
- `actor_display_name`: mutable human-readable label;
- `provider_accounts`: linked external identities such as GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Azure DevOps, or local Git identity;
- `agent_id`: executing agent/model instance or named automation identity;
- `delegation`: relationship showing that an agent acted for a human/service actor;
- `replica_id`: stable machine/worktree writer identity used by distributed ESDB;
- `session_id`: one bounded execution session;
- `identity_confidence` and `verification_state`: explain how the identity was resolved.

Do not use email addresses, Git display names, OS usernames, branch names, or provider usernames as the canonical actor ID by themselves.

## Resolution precedence

Resolve each identity field independently using the configuration precedence from #339:

1. explicit CLI/session override;
2. environment override;
3. project-local identity override;
4. tracked project identity policy or permitted mapping;
5. global Specsmith user identity;
6. verified provider identity auto-detection;
7. local Git identity;
8. OS account fallback;
9. anonymous/generated local identity with a warning.

A lower-confidence source must not silently replace a higher-confidence configured identity.

## Auto-detection providers

Support pluggable detection, initially including:

- GitHub CLI authenticated user when `gh` is available;
- repository GitHub remote owner/account context as supporting evidence only;
- GitLab CLI/account where available;
- Azure DevOps or other supported VCS identity where available;
- `git config user.name` and `user.email` as low-confidence supporting metadata;
- OS username as final low-confidence fallback.

Detection must be read-only, bounded, timeout-safe, and work offline. Missing provider CLIs must not fail normal Specsmith operation.

## Global and local configuration

Global config may contain a default identity profile:

```yaml
identity:
  actor_id: USER-...
  display_name: Example User
  preferred_profile: default
  provider_links:
    github:
      login: example
      account_id: "12345"
      verification: provider-detected
  privacy:
    include_email_in_events: false
```

Project-local `.specsmith/local.yml` may select or override the active profile for that checkout or worktree without changing the tracked project configuration.

Tracked `docs/SPECSMITH.yml` may define identity policy, organization IDs, required trust levels, and allowed actor mappings, but should not force every contributor to share one personal identity.

## Required commands

Add operations such as:

```text
specsmith identity show
specsmith identity detect
specsmith identity explain
specsmith identity list
specsmith identity create <name>
specsmith identity use <name> [--global|--local]
specsmith identity set <field> <value> [--global|--local]
specsmith identity link github
specsmith identity unlink github
specsmith identity verify
specsmith identity rotate-replica
```

`show` and `explain` must display the resolved source, confidence, verification state, delegation, replica, and session identity while redacting private data according to policy.

## Stable ID requirements

1. Generate opaque stable IDs rather than IDs derived directly from names or email addresses.
2. Keep global actor identity stable across repositories and branch changes unless manually rotated.
3. Keep `replica_id` stable for one installation/worktree writer, but allow explicit rotation and document whether clones receive a new replica.
4. New worktrees should receive distinct replica IDs when they can write independently.
5. Session IDs must be unique and non-reused.
6. Agent IDs must distinguish separate agents/models where attribution matters.
7. Identity aliases and provider links may change without rewriting historical events.

## ESDB integration

Every canonical ESDB event introduced by #336 must include resolved identity provenance:

- actor ID and display label at event time;
- executing agent ID and model/provider metadata where applicable;
- delegation relationship;
- replica and session IDs;
- resolution source and verification state;
- optional provider-account references;
- no unnecessary private fields.

Historical records must retain their original attribution even after a user changes their current display name or linked account.

## Trust and privacy

- Provider auto-detection establishes evidence, not absolute identity proof.
- Organization policy may require a configured mapping, signed attestation, or verified account ID for privileged actions.
- Do not expose email by default in committed canonical events.
- Do not store provider tokens or credentials in Specsmith config or ESDB.
- A user may disable provider detection or particular detectors.
- Manual override must be visible in provenance.
- Shared machines must support multiple named identity profiles without overwriting each other.

## Named tests and binary pass/fail conditions

### IDENTITY-001 — GitHub detection

**Pass:** with a mocked/current GitHub CLI account, Specsmith links the account as provider evidence, creates or selects a stable actor profile, and records the detection source.

**Fail:** the login string alone becomes the canonical ID or credentials are persisted.

### IDENTITY-002 — Offline fallback

**Pass:** without network/provider CLIs, configured global identity remains usable; otherwise Git/OS fallback is marked low-confidence with actionable guidance.

**Fail:** normal commands fail solely because provider detection is unavailable.

### IDENTITY-003 — Precedence

**Pass:** explicit > environment > local > project policy/mapping > global > detected provider > Git > OS, with `identity explain` showing each candidate and winner.

**Fail:** a lower layer silently replaces a higher configured identity.

### IDENTITY-004 — Same display name, different users

**Pass:** two profiles with the same display name retain different actor IDs and remain distinguishable after ESDB merge.

**Fail:** profiles collapse because names match.

### IDENTITY-005 — One user, multiple replicas

**Pass:** one actor working in two independent worktrees has one actor ID and two distinct replica IDs; merged events retain both.

**Fail:** replicas are conflated or actor identity changes by worktree.

### IDENTITY-006 — Multiple agents for one user

**Pass:** two agents acting for one human share the delegated actor ID but have distinct agent IDs and session IDs.

**Fail:** agent actions cannot be attributed separately or appear as direct human actions without delegation.

### IDENTITY-007 — Manual override

**Pass:** a user can select a different local/global profile, provenance records the manual source, and historical events remain unchanged.

**Fail:** changing identity rewrites history or silently affects another worktree.

### IDENTITY-008 — Provider account change

**Pass:** linking a new provider account updates the current identity profile without changing actor ID unless the user explicitly creates/selects another actor.

**Fail:** provider username changes create accidental new identities or merge unrelated users.

### IDENTITY-009 — Replica rotation

**Pass:** explicit rotation creates a new replica ID with a recorded predecessor link; old events remain attributable.

**Fail:** rotation rewrites old events or reuses the previous ID.

### IDENTITY-010 — Shared machine profiles

**Pass:** multiple named profiles coexist globally and project-local selection chooses one without modifying the others.

**Fail:** detecting or selecting one user overwrites another user's profile.

### IDENTITY-011 — Privacy

**Pass:** email and private provider metadata are omitted/redacted by default from committed events and normal output.

**Fail:** private fields appear without explicit policy opt-in.

### IDENTITY-012 — Unauthorized claim

**Pass:** an event or environment override claiming another configured actor without required mapping/attestation is marked unverified or blocked according to project policy.

**Fail:** a matching string is treated as verified identity.

### IDENTITY-013 — Cross-platform stability

**Pass:** Linux, Windows, and macOS resolve the same configured actor profile semantics while maintaining platform-local replica identity.

**Fail:** path, case, hostname, or username differences change actor identity unexpectedly.

### IDENTITY-014 — ESDB merge attribution

**Pass:** multi-user events merged under #336 retain actor, agent, replica, session, delegation, and verification fields with deterministic materialized results.

**Fail:** attribution is dropped, overwritten, or changed by merge order.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Centralized identity resolver exists and uses #339 configuration precedence.
- [ ] Actor, agent, replica, and session identities are distinct.
- [ ] GitHub and other provider detection is pluggable, read-only, optional, and explainable.
- [ ] Global defaults and local overrides are supported.
- [ ] Users can manually create, select, modify, link, and inspect identities.
- [ ] ESDB events retain stable multi-user/multi-agent attribution.
- [ ] Private data and credentials are not written by default.
- [ ] IDENTITY-001 through IDENTITY-014 exist and pass with #337 machine-readable evidence.

## Non-goals

- Do not make GitHub mandatory for Specsmith identity.
- Do not claim provider detection proves legal identity.
- Do not use a mutable display name as a primary key.
- Do not store authentication tokens.
- Do not put personal local identity values into tracked project configuration by default.

## Agent execution notes

Implement #339's config resolver first. Keep identity detection adapters pure and independently testable. Reuse the resolved identity object everywhere instead of adding new environment lookups in individual subsystems. Coordinate the event envelope and multi-user tests with #336.

title: Make ESDB branch-merge-safe with replicated events, explicit conflicts, and multi-user identity
body:
## Objective

Make Specsmith ESDB safe for distributed development where multiple users, agents, devices, worktrees, and Git branches independently create epistemic records and later merge them without data loss, silent last-write-wins behavior, broken audit chains, or ambiguous attribution.

This is foundational for #333 and #334 and must be included in epic #335.

## Current-state assessment

The repository already makes two correct choices:

1. `.specsmith/esdb.sqlite3` is ignored and treated as a local rebuildable index rather than a Git-merged database file.
2. Canonical session events use immutable IDs and deterministic set-union merge semantics, rejecting the same event ID with different content.

However, the overall ESDB model is not yet safely distributed:

- `.chronomemory/events.wal` is a single tracked linear sequence/hash chain. Two branches extending the same head create divergent sequence numbers and `prev_hash` values.
- `.chronomemory/snapshot.json` is a shared derived mutable file and can conflict or silently reflect only one branch.
- mutable record IDs are upserted in SQLite with unconditional last-write-wins semantics.
- the audit-event table is one local linear chain and cannot directly merge two independently extended branches.
- actor identity is a display string derived from environment/Git name, not a stable user/agent/device identity.
- no replica identity, branch observation, causal parents, conflict records, or deterministic merge protocol is defined.

## Recommended architecture

Use a **Git-mergeable immutable event set** as the canonical distributed representation and treat SQLite/snapshots/materialized records as local derived views.

Do not attempt to Git-merge SQLite databases or splice two independent linear hash chains.

### Canonical event model

Every mutation produces an immutable canonical event containing at least:

```json
{
  "schema_version": 1,
  "event_id": "content-addressed-or-uuidv7-id",
  "record_id": "REQ-001",
  "operation": "create|revise|supersede|tombstone|link|resolve_conflict",
  "payload": {},
  "payload_hash": "...",
  "actor_id": "USER-...|AGENT-...",
  "agent_id": "AGENT-...",
  "replica_id": "REPLICA-...",
  "session_id": "...",
  "work_item_id": "...",
  "branch_observed": "feature/example",
  "commit_observed": "...",
  "causal_parents": ["EVENT-..."],
  "timestamp_utc": "...",
  "signature_or_attestation": null
}
```

`branch_observed` is provenance only and must not define identity or merge order because branches can be renamed or rebased.

### Storage layout

Prefer one of these Git-friendly layouts, selected through implementation analysis:

1. one immutable event per file under `.chronomemory/events/<prefix>/<event-id>.json`; or
2. append-only shard files partitioned by stable `replica_id`, such as `.chronomemory/replicas/<replica-id>/events.jsonl`.

One-event-per-file provides the cleanest Git set union and conflict isolation. Replica shards are acceptable only if concurrent writers never edit the same shard and deterministic shard repair exists.

The current monolithic WAL may remain as a generated export/compatibility artifact during migration, but it must not remain the authoritative merge surface.

### Integrity model

Replace one global linear chain with independently verifiable immutable events plus deterministic manifests/checkpoints:

- content hash each event;
- optionally maintain per-replica hash chains;
- generate a deterministic Merkle/root manifest over the sorted canonical event set;
- preserve original per-replica chain evidence after merging;
- never rewrite historical event payloads to create an artificial global order;
- materialize a deterministic replay order only for view construction.

### Materialization and conflicts

Rebuild SQLite, snapshots, context indexes, and other views from the merged canonical event set.

For different records, merge by event-set union.

For the same record:

- causally ordered revisions apply normally;
- byte-identical duplicate events deduplicate by event ID/hash;
- concurrent non-overlapping structured-field edits may auto-merge only through an explicit deterministic record-kind policy;
- concurrent conflicting edits must create a first-class `conflict` record preserving both event IDs and both payloads;
- no timestamp-only or branch-order last-write-wins resolution for governance data;
- requirements, tests, user constraints, accepted decisions, tombstones, and security-sensitive records require explicit resolution when concurrent changes conflict.

Tombstones are events. A concurrent revise-versus-tombstone must remain conflicted unless an explicit kind policy says otherwise.

### Stable identity and multiple users

Define stable identities separately:

- `actor_id`: human or service principal responsible for the action;
- `agent_id`: executing model/agent instance where applicable;
- `replica_id`: persistent installation/worktree writer identity;
- `session_id`: one bounded execution session;
- Git author/committer metadata: supporting provenance, not the primary identity.

Requirements:

- generate a stable local `replica_id` outside tracked project state or through a user-specific ignored config;
- allow explicit organization-managed actor IDs;
- never infer two users are the same solely from display name;
- preserve human-on-behalf-of-agent attribution;
- prevent one user from silently overwriting another user’s unresolved conflicting evidence;
- support optional event signing/attestation without making signatures mandatory for local use.

### Merge and rebuild commands

Add portable operations such as:

```text
specsmith esdb merge-check
specsmith esdb rebuild
specsmith esdb conflicts
specsmith esdb resolve <conflict-id>
specsmith esdb verify-distributed
```

`merge-check` must be read-only and suitable for CI. `rebuild` must regenerate all derived state deterministically.

## Migration requirements

1. Import the current monolithic ChronoMemory WAL without losing hashes, sequence information, or provenance.
2. Assign legacy events to a stable migration replica and preserve the original chain as evidence.
3. Stop tracking mutable SQLite databases.
4. Decide whether `snapshot.json` becomes ignored/generated or a deterministic reviewed artifact. It must never be authoritative.
5. Maintain backward-compatible read/export support for the existing WAL format during a documented transition.
6. Detect and reject malformed duplicate event IDs with different payload hashes.
7. Make migration idempotent and resumable.

## Required test cases and binary pass/fail conditions

### ESDB-DIST-001 — Independent branch additions

Given branches A and B from the same base, each adds ten unique events.

**Pass:** merged canonical store contains all 20 events exactly once; distributed verification passes; both SQLite and snapshot rebuilds contain all records.

**Fail:** any event is missing, duplicated, silently replaced, or verification depends on merge order.

### ESDB-DIST-002 — Identical duplicate event

Both branches contain the exact same event ID and bytes.

**Pass:** merge produces one logical event and no conflict.

**Fail:** duplicate records appear or the merge is rejected.

### ESDB-DIST-003 — Event-ID collision with different payload

Branches contain the same event ID with different payload hashes.

**Pass:** merge-check fails closed with an explicit collision diagnostic; neither payload is discarded.

**Fail:** one payload wins, both are treated as one, or rebuild proceeds as healthy.

### ESDB-DIST-004 — Concurrent same-record conflicting revision

Two users revise the same authoritative requirement field from the same causal parent.

**Pass:** both revisions survive and a first-class unresolved conflict blocks use of a single resolved value.

**Fail:** timestamp, Git merge order, actor priority, or SQLite upsert silently selects one.

### ESDB-DIST-005 — Concurrent non-overlapping revision

Two branches change different mergeable fields under an explicit record-kind policy.

**Pass:** deterministic merged materialization is identical regardless of input order and retains both source event IDs.

**Fail:** result changes with replay order or provenance is lost.

### ESDB-DIST-006 — Revise versus tombstone

One branch revises a record while another tombstones it from the same parent.

**Pass:** conflict remains explicit until resolved and both operations remain auditable.

**Fail:** deletion or revision silently wins.

### ESDB-DIST-007 — Multi-user attribution

Two humans and two agents write from three replicas with overlapping sessions.

**Pass:** every event retains correct actor, agent, replica, and session attribution; same display names do not collapse identities.

**Fail:** attribution is missing, ambiguous, or derived only from mutable Git display names.

### ESDB-DIST-008 — Rebase and branch rename

Events are created, then their branch is renamed and commits rebased.

**Pass:** event identity and merge outcome remain unchanged; observed Git metadata remains provenance only.

**Fail:** event IDs or causal identity depend on branch name or commit SHA alone.

### ESDB-DIST-009 — Three-way and octopus merge convergence

Three replicas independently add and revise records, then merge in every permutation.

**Pass:** all permutations produce the same canonical event-set root, conflict set, and materialized view digest.

**Fail:** any permutation produces a different healthy result.

### ESDB-DIST-010 — Interrupted writer and malformed shard

A partially written event/shard is introduced.

**Pass:** atomic writes prevent normal partial files; verification quarantines malformed artifacts and refuses a healthy rebuild without deleting evidence.

**Fail:** malformed data is silently skipped while reporting success.

### ESDB-DIST-011 — Legacy WAL migration

Migrate a fixture containing the existing linear WAL and snapshot.

**Pass:** every legacy event is preserved, original chain verification remains available, migration is idempotent, and derived view digest is stable.

**Fail:** sequence/hash provenance is lost or a second migration changes the event set.

### ESDB-DIST-012 — Backend parity

Replay the same merged canonical event fixture into SQLite and ChronoStore.

**Pass:** logical records, statuses, conflicts, provenance links, and context-visible results match.

**Fail:** backend selection changes governance semantics.

### ESDB-DIST-013 — Cross-platform merge

Generate events on Linux, Windows, and macOS using paths and identities containing spaces and Unicode.

**Pass:** canonical hashes, event-set root, and rebuilt logical state are identical.

**Fail:** newline, path, encoding, or case behavior changes event identity.

### ESDB-DIST-014 — Unauthorized identity substitution

An event claims another configured actor without valid delegation/attestation.

**Pass:** policy marks attribution unverified or rejects it according to configured trust mode; original bytes remain evidence.

**Fail:** claimed actor is accepted as verified solely because it is a string field.

### ESDB-DIST-015 — Conflict resolution

Resolve a first-class conflict with an explicit resolution event referencing all alternatives.

**Pass:** resolution is auditable, does not erase alternatives, and all replicas converge after merging the resolution event.

**Fail:** resolution rewrites history or only updates one local database.

## CI requirements

- run deterministic merge permutations;
- run SQLite tests unconditionally;
- run ChronoStore parity where licensed/available and maintain contract fixtures otherwise;
- run Linux, Windows, and macOS canonicalization tests;
- fail CI on unresolved collisions, malformed canonical events, non-convergent roots, or stale derived artifacts;
- report conflict IDs and involved event IDs without dumping secrets.

## Non-goals

- Do not merge SQLite files through Git.
- Do not create a fake global linear history by rewriting branch events.
- Do not use wall-clock timestamps as the sole conflict resolver.
- Do not silently collapse multiple users or agents into one actor.
- Do not make a mutable snapshot the source of truth.
- Do not require an always-online central server for normal Git-based collaboration.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Canonical ESDB state merges by immutable event-set union.
- [ ] Derived SQLite and snapshot state rebuild deterministically from canonical events.
- [ ] The existing monolithic linear WAL is no longer the sole authoritative merge surface.
- [ ] Concurrent same-record edits never resolve through silent last-write-wins.
- [ ] Stable actor, agent, replica, and session identities are captured.
- [ ] All ESDB-DIST-001 through ESDB-DIST-015 tests exist and pass.
- [ ] Merge order and platform do not change canonical roots or healthy materialized state.
- [ ] Legacy WAL migration preserves original chain evidence.
- [ ] Unresolved conflicts are queryable and block unsafe authoritative reads.
- [ ] Documentation explains branch, worktree, multi-user, merge, rebuild, and conflict-resolution workflows.

## Agent execution notes

Start with an architecture decision record and fixtures demonstrating the current divergent-WAL failure. Keep immutable canonical events separate from local materialized indexes. Coordinate schema changes with #333 context residency and #334 context-health records so they use the same distributed event envelope.

title: Epic: Zoo Code local-agent defaults and epistemic context lifecycle
body:
## Goal

Make Specsmith-generated Zoo Code environments immediately usable with a local LiteLLM proxy while proactively managing context as a governed, evidence-backed working set rather than an ever-growing transcript.

The finished system must:

- default Zoo Code to a local LiteLLM profile with prompt caching;
- allow all auto-execute commands by default while preserving user customization;
- predict whether new work fits before execution;
- create cheap checkpoints without excessive token use;
- preserve important state before condensation;
- offload durable information to ESDB/ChronoMemory;
- load only relevant, authoritative evidence into active model context;
- detect and clean contaminated context without deleting evidence;
- start a fresh bounded task when the current context cannot be made safe.

## Child tickets and implementation order

### Zoo defaults

- [ ] #329 — Default Zoo Code auto-approved executable commands to wildcard
- [ ] #331 — Generate a default Zoo Code Local-LLM LiteLLM profile with prompt caching enabled

These may proceed in parallel but must share one ownership/preservation model for Zoo settings.

### Context decision layer

- [ ] #332 — Add predictive context-risk preflight and dynamic governance checkpoint policy

This establishes telemetry, task-envelope estimation, micro-checkpoints, semantic checkpoints, pressure states, condensation decisions, and fresh-task fallback.

### Durable context and retrieval

- [ ] #333 — Implement ESDB-backed context residency, offloading, and smart rehydration

This separates active-context residency from durable evidence lifecycle, fixes destructive eviction semantics, migrates session continuity toward structured ESDB records, and introduces bounded retrieval.

### Context integrity and cleanup

- [ ] #334 — Add epistemic context-health checks and governed context cleanup

This detects contamination, staleness, contradiction, provenance loss, duplication, and missing invariants, then rebuilds a verified working packet from authoritative sources.

### Platform completion gate

- [ ] #330 — Audit and harden Specsmith release/bootstrap workflows across Linux, Windows, and macOS

Apply its platform requirements to all Zoo settings, context storage, file paths, virtual environments, temporary state, encodings, and tests introduced by this epic.

## Architectural model

### Zoo Code owns

- live provider/model context telemetry;
- actual context condensation;
- task creation and boomerang returns;
- API profile persistence and mode/profile association;
- execution approval behavior;
- prompt-cache request/usage support exposed by the provider;
- user-visible warnings and controls.

### Specsmith owns

- governance and checkpoint policy;
- task-fit estimation;
- context pressure states;
- semantic checkpoint schema;
- durable ESDB/ChronoMemory evidence;
- context residency and retrieval ranking;
- source authority and provenance;
- context health and cleanup policy;
- post-condensation integrity verification;
- fresh-task handoff requirements.

### ESDB/ChronoMemory owns

- durable records and immutable/provenance-linked events;
- decisions, constraints, requirements, tests, evidence, checkpoints, handoffs, and health events;
- retrieval indexes and graph relationships;
- long-term state that does not need continuous residence in the LLM context.

## Required invariants

1. **Leaving context is not deletion.** Removing a record from the active LLM packet must not tombstone or delete durable evidence.
2. **Active constraints remain explicit.** Exact user constraints, accepted decisions, current proposal, active requirements, critical risks, and next action remain pinned while relevant.
3. **Summaries are references, not authority.** A summary or handoff must retain source IDs and cannot replace exact authoritative evidence when precision matters.
4. **No large task starts blindly.** Medium/high tasks receive task-fit preflight before broad reads, edits, or expensive tools.
5. **Condensation is transactional.** Checkpoint first, condense, verify required state, recalculate capacity, then continue or start a fresh task.
6. **Cleanup is non-destructive.** Context cleanup changes residency and the working packet, not durable evidence lifecycle state.
7. **Contradictions remain visible.** Conflicting evidence is preserved and labeled until resolved by authoritative evidence or explicit decision.
8. **Settings preserve user intent.** Specsmith may migrate its untouched generated defaults but must not silently overwrite user-edited Zoo profiles, mappings, caching choices, or command allowlists.
9. **No secrets in generated settings.** Local-LLM configuration contains no real API key.
10. **Cross-platform behavior is real.** Generated settings and context lifecycle tests pass on Linux, Windows, and macOS.

## Default operational flow

```text
User requests work
  -> context health check
  -> proposal and task-envelope estimate
  -> compare envelope with usable remaining capacity
  -> micro-checkpoint material state
  -> if safe: load bounded relevant context and execute
  -> if pressured: semantic checkpoint and Zoo condensation
  -> verify checkpoint/invariants and recalculate
  -> if still unsafe: create fresh bounded Zoo task
  -> execute and verify
  -> persist evidence/checkpoint/health telemetry to ESDB
  -> release irrelevant residency while preserving durable records
```

## Default context policy

Use configurable defaults approximately equivalent to:

```yaml
context_governance:
  checkpoint:
    token_growth_trigger_pct: 12
    min_meaningful_turns: 6
    max_meaningful_turns: 12
    material_event_trigger: 5
    failure_trigger: 3
    cooldown_turns: 4
  pressure:
    yellow_pct_of_usable: 50
    orange_pct_of_usable: 65
    red_pct_of_usable: 78
  preflight:
    uncertainty_margin_pct: 25
    minimum_post_task_headroom_pct: 20
  actions:
    yellow: checkpoint_at_boundary
    orange: checkpoint_then_evaluate
    red: checkpoint_condense_recalculate
    insufficient_after_condense: spawn_fresh_task
```

Tune through measured telemetry rather than treating these as immutable constants.

## Program-level acceptance criteria

- [ ] New Zoo setup creates `Local-LLM` at `http://127.0.0.1:4000`, maps every Specsmith mode to it, and enables supported prompt caching.
- [ ] New Zoo setup uses a literal wildcard as the sole generated auto-execute allowlist entry.
- [ ] User-edited Zoo settings survive upgrade/sync.
- [ ] A large task arriving near 70% context is intercepted before execution and either safely condensed or delegated to a fresh task.
- [ ] Micro-checkpointing does not require an LLM call.
- [ ] Semantic checkpoints preserve exact constraints, evidence, decisions, failures, and next action.
- [ ] ESDB-backed context offloading reduces active context without deleting/tombstoning evidence.
- [ ] Session resume loads a bounded relevant packet rather than a large raw transcript.
- [ ] Context-health checks detect stale, contradictory, duplicate, unsupported, or provenance-deficient content.
- [ ] Governed cleanup can rebuild a verified clean packet and refuses to continue when critical information is missing.
- [ ] Context retrieval and cleanup are auditable and explainable.
- [ ] Linux, Windows, and macOS integration tests cover generation, migration, checkpointing, condensation handoff, offload/rehydration, cleanup, and fresh-task fallback.

## Instructions for local AI agents

1. Read this epic and the assigned child ticket in full.
2. Inspect current Zoo Code schema and current Specsmith implementations before editing; do not trust old example field names.
3. Implement deterministic, testable policy functions before wiring them to Zoo or LLM calls.
4. Never use ESDB deletion/tombstoning to mean temporary context eviction.
5. Never replace exact user constraints or accepted decisions with an unverified prose summary.
6. Keep public/user customization preservation explicit and tested.
7. Add migration tests for prior generated settings and legacy session files.
8. Do not add broad LLM health checks to every turn; use deterministic gates and bounded semantic review.
9. Do not tag, publish, force-push, or modify branch protection while implementing these tickets.
10. Keep PRs aligned to child-ticket boundaries unless an interface must land atomically.

## Suggested PR order

1. #329 and #331
2. #332
3. #333, beginning with non-destructive residency correction
4. #334
5. #330 cross-platform completion and end-to-end matrix

Each PR must leave existing behavior passing and document any temporary interface dependency.
